cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: MoEngage acquires Aampe to bring 1:1 agentic decisioning to B2C teams
stories.plain: MoEngage bought a company whose whole idea is one AI agent per customer — not one model per segment. Each agent decides what to message a single person, when, how often and where, and keeps learning from what worked. The pitch: personalization stops hitting the “not enough humans to build all the journeys” ceiling.

stories.head: Salesforce pushes agentic commerce at Cannes: a Shopper Agent from discovery to checkout
stories.plain: Salesforce wants brands to put an AI “Shopper Agent” on their own sites that walks a buyer all the way from browsing to paying. The number doing the talking: traffic referred by AI reportedly converts far better than social. The shift is from omnichannel (be everywhere a human clicks) to agentic commerce (sell to the customer’s AI).

stories.head: Forrester: nine in ten US agencies use generative AI, half now use agentic AI
stories.plain: New Cannes research says adoption is near-total — but flags a trap: teams are mostly using AI to cut cost, not to make better work. That’s the efficiency-vs-effectiveness tension, and it’s the thing a strategy team has to actively manage, because the tools won’t do it for you.

stories.head: Zeta rebuilds its Data Cloud on Palantir Foundry to power governed agentic marketing
stories.plain: Zeta is moving its customer-data engine onto Palantir’s platform so its AI can reach more enterprise data while keeping governance and control over it. The tell for a regulated reader: the headline feature isn’t smarter targeting, it’s being able to prove the data is governed. Governance is becoming the product.

stories.head: SAP Engagement Cloud bets real-time engagement decides who wins in 2026
stories.plain: SAP’s argument: batch-and-blast is dead; the edge now is reacting to a customer in the moment. It’s the same thread as the MoEngage deal — the whole category is racing toward decisions made per-person, in real time, by software.

stories.head: Salesforce’s buying spree for agentic platforms continues as Intercom becomes Fin
stories.plain: The big platforms are buying agentic capability rather than waiting to build it. For a strategist the signal matters more than the deal: consolidation this fast means “agentic” has moved from demo to must-own.

stories.head: Adobe uses its Semrush acquisition to launch a GEO tool with AI agents
stories.plain: Adobe shipped a tool whose agents recommend and make changes to help a brand show up inside LLM answers. This is GEO going mainstream — the new fight isn’t ranking on Google, it’s being the brand an AI mentions.

stories.head: Bloomreach’s Loomi agent turns a single prompt into a full campaign workflow
stories.plain: Type what you want; the agent builds the whole campaign flow. Useful, and also a reminder of the commodity line: generating a campaign is becoming table stakes — the moat is in deciding and governing, not drafting.

stories.head: Asana reframes itself as an “operating system for human-agent teams”
stories.plain: Asana’s new suite has humans and AI agents working off the same plan, same context, same governance. The interesting word is governance again — vendors now lead with control, not just speed.

stories.head: ActiveCampaign’s update teaches its AI to remember your brand identity
stories.plain: The tool now holds onto voice guidelines, colours and logos across sessions so drafts stay on-brand. Small feature, real theme: brand consistency is the quiet bottleneck once everyone can generate content instantly.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Agentic decisioning went from buzzword to balance sheet
body: A month ago “agents that act for the user” was a demo. This month it’s M&A. MoEngage bought Aampe for per-user decisioning; Salesforce kept buying agentic platforms; Asana rebuilt around human-agent teams; Aircall acquired Piper for agentic sales.

The signal: the market has decided agentic decisioning is infrastructure you own, not a feature you wait for. The question shifts from “should we?” to “build, buy or partner — and how do we govern it?”

themes.head: A new optimization target appeared: being cited by the AI
body: Adobe shipped a GEO tool; Google added AI-visibility metrics; DISQO launched a way to measure ad impact inside LLM answers. Separate announcements, one shift: marketers now optimize to be the brand an AI mentions, not just the link a human clicks.

GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) is becoming its own discipline. If you only know SEO, this is the concept to add first — the playbook rhymes, but the surface is the model’s answer, not the results page.

themes.head: The mood turned from experimentation to accountability
body: Budgets are up (Stensul: ~79% of orgs increasing MarTech spend) but the framing changed. The State of Martech report calls this the “Darwin phase”; Gartner expects 40%+ of agentic projects cancelled by 2027; the recurring verdict is that capability is no longer the constraint — enablement and governance are.
This is the most important theme to grasp, because it explains the actual job. The bottleneck isn’t the model. It’s whether the org can train people, redesign workflow, and prove value safely.

themes.head: The customer is becoming an agent: omnichannel gives way to agentic commerce
body: Salesforce put a Shopper Agent on brand storefronts; analysts now talk about a meaningful share of interactions happening agent-to-agent, where a shopper’s AI assistant negotiates with a brand’s AI. Retail is reframing from omnichannel (be everywhere a human clicks) to agentic commerce (sell to the customer’s software).
For a strategist the uncomfortable question is what your brand still owns when discovery, comparison and checkout are handled by an agent. That’s a positioning problem, not a tooling one.

themes.head: AI started eating SaaS: teams replace tools with built alternatives
body: A counter-narrative gained ground: rather than AI features bolted onto SaaS, marketers are replacing point tools with AI-built alternatives, driving churn and shifting where value sits. “Vibe coding” can stand up a replacement fast — though security, integration and maintenance risk can erase the savings.
The lesson for buyers: the moat is no longer feature count, it’s the data, workflows and governance a tool is wired into. Easy to rebuild a feature; hard to rebuild the context around it.

month.takeaway: In MarTech AI right now, the constraint isn’t capability — it’s governing demand, decisioning and visibility well enough to ship.
